coredata.dc:title: Fluorescence detection of mercury ions and cysteine based on magnesium and nitrogen co-doped carbon quantum dots and IMPLICATION logic gate operation
coredata.dc:creator: Liu, Ting
Li, Na
Dong, Jiang Xue
Luo, Hong Qun
Li, Nian Bing
coredata.dc:description: Novel magnesium and nitrogen co-doped carbon quantum dots (Mg-N-CQDs) were developed as a fluorescent switch for sensitive and selective sensing of Hg(II) and cysteine (Cys). The Mg-N-CQDs synthesized by hydrothermal method possess high fluorescence intensity. However, after the addition of Hg(II), the fluorescence of Mg-N-CQDs aqueous solution was dramatically quenched, which may be attributed to the formation of non-fluorescent complex between Mg-N-CQDs and Hg(II). Interestingly, the fluorescence of the Mg-N-CQDs aqueous solution containing Hg(II) could be recovered gradually in the presence of Cys, due to the stronger binding affinity of Hg(II) toward Cys than toward Mg-N-CQDs. Based on these phenomena, a fluorescent probe for detection of Hg(II) and Cys was explored, and further applied to detect Hg(II) in real water samples with satisfactory results. Moreover, an IMPLICATION logic gate was constructed using Hg(II)/Cys as inputs and the fluorescence signal of Mg-N-CQDs as an output.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Mg-N-CQDs
Hg(II)
Fluorescence
Cysteine
IMPLICATION logic gate

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Synthesis of magnesium and nitrogen co-doped carbon quantum dots
Fluorescence sensing of Hg(II) and cysteine

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic illustration of the synthesis of Mg-N-CQDs, as well as the Hg(II) and cysteine detection based on Mg-N-CQDs.

(A) TEM and (B) HRTEM of Mg-N-CQDs. Inset to (A): size distribution of Mg-N-CQDs. Inset to (B): local lattice amplification of the single Mg-N-CQDs. (C) XPS full scan spectrum, (D) C1s and (E) N1s scan spectra of Mg-N-CQDs.

(A) UV–vis absorption spectra of three different CQDs in aqueous solution. Inset in panel (A) shows the photographs of three types of CQDs in aqueous solution under natural light and UV light (365nm). (B) Fluorescence emission spectra of Mg-N-CQDs at different excitation wavelengths. Inset shows the fluorescence excitation and emission spectra of Mg-N-CQD in aqueous solutions (λ ex =348nm, λ em =440nm).

(A) Fluorescence spectra of Mg-N-CQDs in aqueous solution in the presence of different concentrations of Hg(II) (from top to bottom: 0.05, 0.075, 0.2, 0.4, 0.8, 1, 2, 3, 4, and 5μM). (B) The relative fluorescence intensity, F 0 − F 1 (F 0 and F 1 represent the fluorescence of the system of Mg-N-CQDs and Mg-N-CQDs/Hg(II), respectively), is dependent on the concentration of Hg(II) ions within the range of 0–25μM, and the inset shows the linear range for Hg(II) is 0.05–5μM.

Fluorescence quenching efficiency of the aqueous Mg-N-CQDs solution in the presence of various metal ions. F 0 is the fluorescence intensity of Mg-N-CQDs, F 1 refers to the fluorescence intensity of the Mg-N-CQDs system with various metal ions. The concentration of Hg(II) ion is 5μM and that of other metal ions is 15μM.

The relationship between fluorescence of Mg-N-CQDs/Hg(II) and various amino acids. F 2 is the recovered fluorescence intensity of Mg-N-CQDs/Hg(II) in the presence of Cys, F 1 is the fluorescence intensity of Mg-N-CQDs in the presence of Hg(II) (15μM), and F 0 is the fluorescence intensity of Mg-N-CQDs at 348nm, respectively. The concentration of Cys is 15μM, and that of other amino acids is 25μM.
Truth Table (A), symbol (B), and reversible switching (C) of the IMPLICATION logic gate.
Analytical results of Hg(II) in tap water and Jialing river samples (n =3).

head.ce:title: Fluorescence detection of mercury ions and cysteine based on magnesium and nitrogen co-doped carbon quantum dots and IMPLICATION logic gate operation

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Key Laboratory of Eco-environments in Three Gorges Reservoir Region (Ministry of Education), School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Southwest University, Chongqing 400715, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory of Eco-environments in Three Gorges Reservoir Region (Ministry of Education)
School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Novel magnesium and nitrogen co-doped carbon quantum dots (Mg-N-CQDs) were developed as a fluorescent switch for sensitive and selective sensing of Hg(II) and cysteine (Cys). The Mg-N-CQDs synthesized by hydrothermal method possess high fluorescence intensity. However, after the addition of Hg(II), the fluorescence of Mg-N-CQDs aqueous solution was dramatically quenched, which may be attributed to the formation of non-fluorescent complex between Mg-N-CQDs and Hg(II). Interestingly, the fluorescence of the Mg-N-CQDs aqueous solution containing Hg(II) could be recovered gradually in the presence of Cys, due to the stronger binding affinity of Hg(II) toward Cys than toward Mg-N-CQDs. Based on these phenomena, a fluorescent probe for detection of Hg(II) and Cys was explored, and further applied to detect Hg(II) in real water samples with satisfactory results. Moreover, an IMPLICATION logic gate was constructed using Hg(II)/Cys as inputs and the fluorescence signal of Mg-N-CQDs as an output.

ce:section.ce:para: Carbon quantum dots (CQDs) are a new class of carbon nonmaterials emerged in recent years and were discovered coincidently during the purification of single-walled carbon nanotubes (SWNTs) in 2004 and named in 2006 [1–3]. In contrast to semiconductor quantum dots and organic dyes, CQDs have attracted increasing attention because of their outstanding fluorescence properties, high photostability, good biocompatibility, and low toxicity [1,4–7]. Currently, some methods for the preparation of CQDs have been developed, such as laser-ablation [8,9], electrochemistry [10,11], and combustion/thermal [12,13] and microwave [14] routes, which can be generally classified into two main groups: top-down and bottom-up methods [1,6]. In previous researches, the synthesis of CQDs usually needed strong acid or alkali to gain strong fluorescence [1,15]. Later, the fluorescent CQDs were directly synthesized from ethanol by a one-​step method with simplicity [16]. Thereafter, some inexpensive and biocompatible materials used for the preparation of CQDs were adopted, such as citrate, orange juice, chicken eggs, chitosan, etc. Most of these CQDs have prominent performance in cell imaging and chemical sensing applications [6]. To further improve the fluorescence properties and enlarge the application range of CQDs, surface functionalization and heteroatom doping are two kinds of commonly modified ways. Especially, doping with nitrogen, sulfur, and metal elements plays a significant role in fluorescence enhancement of CQDs. For example, doping with nitrogen can lead to substantial fluorescence enhancement and improve the quantum yield of functionalized CQDs [1,6,17,18].

Mercury is one of the most hazardous pollutants with the feature of strong toxicity and bioaccumulation and can cause serious human health problems including brain damage, kidney failure, vision and hearing loss, and death [19–21]. Therefore, obtaining cost-effective, rapid, and applicable methods for the detection of Hg(II) in the natural environment are in great demand [20]. Traditional methods for Hg(II) detection, such as atomic absorption spectroscopy, inductively coupled plasma-mass spectrometry, gas chromatography, and X-ray fluorescence spectrometry [19,20,22] are highly selective and sensitive but require complicated sample preparation and sophisticated instrumentation. Besides, they cannot be applied to on-site detection of Hg(II) in the environment [19,22]. Recently, small fluorescent organic molecules, DNAzymes, proteins, noble metal-based nanomaterials, quantum dots, etc., have been employed for the detection of Hg(II) [23–29]. However, most of these strategies require multiple steps, and some of materials even have serious toxicity. Cysteine (Cys), as an essential amino acid, is involved in protein synthesis, detoxification, and metabolism. Elevated levels of Cys have been associated with neurotoxicity [10,30], and Cys deficiency is associated with slow growth, hair depigmentation, edema, liver damage, skin lesions, and weakness [30,31].

In this work, we adopt an efficient one-step strategy to synthesize Mg–N co-doped CQDs by hydrothermal method, and the products of this strategy have high quantum yield. Citric acid was used as a carbon source to prepare CQDs. Magnesium hydroxide (Mg(OH)2) was chosen as a chelation agent to protect the carboxyl groups of citric acid during the procedure of dehydration and carbonization. And ethylenediamine (EDA) was able to provide nitrogen atoms as a surface passivation reagent [32,33]. Considering that EDA is volatile, the dosage of EDA was reduced to 0.5μL in this system, which was much lower than that in the reported work [32]. To demonstrate the unique properties of Mg–N co-doped CQDs (Mg-N-CQDs), Mg-doped CQDs (Mg-CQDs) and N-doped CQDs (N-CQDs) were synthesized for comparison. As shown in Scheme 1 , citric acid, EDA, and Mg(OH)2 reacted to produce polymer-like carbon quantum dots (CQDs), which were then carbonized to form the Mg-N-CQDs. And the synthesis processes of N-CQDs and Mg-CQDs are illustrated in Fig. S1. When Hg(II) was introduced into the Mg-N-CQDs solution, the fluorescence of Mg-N-CQDs was greatly quenched due to the electron-transfer process (turn off). However, when Cys was added, the recovery of the fluorescence of the system was observed (turn on). Based on the fluorescence change caused by Hg(II) and Cys, a novel sensor for sensitive and selective detection of Hg(II) and Cys was developed.

Citric acid, ethylenediamine (EDA, 98%), quinine sulfate (98%, suitable for fluorescence), and sulfuric acid (H2SO4, A.R. 98%) were purchased from Aladdin Reagent Co., Ltd., China. Cysteine and other 19 amino acids including alanine (Ala), arginine (Arg), asparagine (Asn), aspartic acid (Asp), glutamic acid (Glu), glycine (Gly), glutamine (Gln), histidine (His), isoleucine (Ile), leucine (Leu), lysine (Lys), methionine (Met), phenylalanine (Phe), proline (Pro), serine (Ser), threonine (Thr), tryptophan (Trp), tyrosine (Tyr), and valine (Val) were purchased from Aldrich. The metal salts used, including Hg(NO3)2·H2O, AgNO3, Al(NO3)3·9H2O, Ca(NO3)2·4H2O, Cd(NO3)2·4H2O, Co(NO3)2·6H2O, Cu(NO3)2·3H2O, Fe(NO3)3·9H2O, FeSO4·7H2O, KNO3, Mg(NO3)2·6H2O, Mg(OH)2, Mn(NO3)2 (50wt.% in H2O), NaNO3, Ni(NO3)2·6H2O, Pb(NO3)2, and Zn(NO3)2·6H2O, were of analytical reagent grade and were used as received. Britton–Robinson (BR) buffers (10mM, pH 3–9) were prepared by mixing 0.2M NaOH and a mixture of 0.04M H3PO4, H3BO3, and CH3COOH according to suitable proportion. All the solutions were prepared with ultrapure water (18.2MΩcm).

The fluorescence measurements were recorded using an F-2700 fluorescence spectrophotometer (Hitachi, Japan). The slit width was set at 10.0nm for both excitation and emission, and the PMT voltage was 400V. The UV–vis absorption spectra of the system were recorded using a UV-2450 spectrophotometer (Shimadzu, Japan). High-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM) images were collected from a JEOL-2100 system (Japan) operated at 200kV. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) spectra were recorded using an ESCALAB 250Xi X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (Thermo Electron, USA). Nuclear magnetic resonance (NMR) spectra were collected on a Bruker AVANCE III 600 (600MHz) (Bruker, Germany) with the freeze dried product dissolved in D2O. Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectra were conducted on a Bruker IFS 113v spectrometer (Germany).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of magnesium and nitrogen co-doped carbon quantum dots
ce:section.ce:para: The magnesium and nitrogen co-doped carbon quantum dots (Mg-N-CQDs) were prepared based on a previous report [32] with some modification. Firstly, 0.4000g of citric acid and 0.1668g of Mg(OH)2 were dissolved in 16mL of ultrapure water. Secondly, 0.5μL of ethylenediamine (EDA) was added. Then, the mixture was transferred to a poly(tetrafluoroethylene) (Teflon)-lined autoclave (25mL). Subsequently, the aqueous solution was heated at 200°C for 3h. After the reaction, the reactor was cooled down to room temperature naturally. The pale yellow products were centrifuged at 12000rpm for 30min. At last, powdered Mg-N-CQDs were derived after dialysis and lyophilization. Simultaneously, two groups of parallel experiments were carried out. N-doped CQDs were synthesized without Mg(OH)2, and Mg-doped CQDs were synthesized without addition of EDA. The compositions of different carbon sources are shown in Table S1.

The quantum yield (QY) of the Mg-N-CQDs was determined via a slope method introduced by Zhu et al. [33]. Quinine sulfate in 0.1M H2SO4 (its quantum yield in literature is 0.54) was chosen as a standard to measure the quantum yield of Mg-N-CQDs. In this paper, five concentrations of quinine sulfate and Mg-N-CQDs solutions were prepared, and all of them had absorbance less than 0.05 at 348nm. The graph was plotted using the integrated fluorescence intensity against the absorbance value. Then the following equation was used to calculate the quantum yield:
(1)
ϕ x = ϕ st (K x/K st)(η x/η st)2
where ϕ is the quantum yield, K is the slope, and η is the refractive index. The subscript “st” refers to the standard, and “x” refers to the samples. For these aqueous solutions, η x/η st =1.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence sensing of Hg(II) and cysteine
ce:section.ce:para: The sensing solutions of Hg(II) were prepared by mixing 40μL of Mg-N-CQDs (1mgmL−1), 450μL of 10mM BR buffer (pH 5.33) and 10μL of Hg(II) with different concentrations. The final Hg(II) concentration in aqueous solution was varied from 0 to 25μM and the concentration of Mg-N-CQDs was kept at 0.08mgmL−1. After reaction for 10min at room temperature, the fluorescence spectra were recorded in the wavelength range of 368–600nm at the maximum excitation wavelength of 348nm. The sensing solutions of Cys with different concentrations were prepared based on the Mg-N-CQDs/Hg(II) solutions containing 15μM Hg(II), and kept to react for 10min. The fluorescence spectra of the system were recorded in the same way mentioned above.

The Mg-N-CQDs mentioned above were characterized using high-resolution transmission electron microscopy for the morphology and size distributions. Fig. 1 A shows the representative TEM image of the Mg-N-CQDs, which exhibits a narrow size distribution around 2–8nm (Fig. 1A, inset), with an average diameter of 5.2nm. The HRTEM image (Fig. 1B) demonstrates that few Mg-N-CQDs contain some structured crystalline in their surface. The lattice spacings of 0.23 and 0.25nm (Fig. 1B, inset) are close to the (100) facet of graphite [7,18]. These surface defects have effects on electronic transitions which are related to the optical properties, like bright photoluminescence emissions [4]. The X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy full scan spectrum (Fig. 1C) shows four obvious peaks ascribed to carbon, nitrogen, oxygen, and magnesium. The inset of Fig. 1C presents the contents of four elements in the sample, which were calculated to be 71.57% (C), 0.71% (N), 23.87% (O), and 3.85% (Mg), respectively, indicating the successful introduction of nitrogen and magnesium into the CQDs. The C1s scan spectrum (Fig. 1D) exhibits an obvious characteristic peak at 284.7eV, confirming the existence of CC and CH in the Mg-N-CQDs. The peak at 286.6eV suggests the presence of CO, and the peak at 288.7eV could be assigned to CO, CN, and OCO [19,32]. The N1s scan spectrum (Fig. 1E) reveals two peaks at 399.5 and 400.6eV, which can be respectively corresponded to pyridinic-like N and pyrrolic-like N [18,34]. The binding energy at around 50eV in Mg2p scan spectrum (Fig. S2) indicates an atomic state of Mg–CO3 [32].

Moreover, 1H nuclear magnetic resonance spectrum was employed to detect sp2 carbons (Fig. S3A). In the 13C NMR spectrum (Fig. S3B), signals in the range of 40–80ppm and 90–180ppm are indicative of aliphatic (sp3) and aromatic (sp2) carbon atoms, respectively. The peaks around 180ppm most probably arise from carboxyl/amide groups [33,35]. Fourier transform infrared spectra (Fig. S4) were used to further identify the functional groups present on Mg-N-CQDs: compared to the broad peak at 3374cm−1 of the OH stretching vibration in citric acid (curve a), Mg-N-CQDs (curve b) have a narrower peak at 3132cm−1 of the NH stretching vibration, which demonstrated the combination of COOH and NH2. What’s more, the peaks at 1587 and 1402cm−1 of Mg-N-CQDs are the stretching vibration of COO− and the CO stretching vibration at 1726cm−1 of citric acid disappeared, indicating that citric acid reacted with Mg(OH)2 to form carboxylate. The peak at 1263cm−1 (curve b) is the stretching vibration of CN, and all of these structures (NH, CN and COO−) provided the surface status information on Mg-N-CQDs.

The fluorescence emission spectra of three types of CQDs in aqueous solutions are presented in Fig. S5. The Mg-N-CQDs possess a very strong fluorescence at 450nm compared to Mg-CQDs and N-CQDs. Obviously, Mg–N double doping can make a great contribution to remarkable fluorescence enhancement of CQDs. In this work, the dosage of raw material was decreased greatly, especially the use of EDA considering its volatility. However, the quantum yield of the Mg-N-CQDs was still very high, which was calculated to be 58.8% (Fig. S6) according to Eq. (1) by using quinine sulfate as a reference. The high quantum yield and strong fluorescence of Mg-N-CQDs suggesting that the nitrogen and magnesium species play a key role in fluorescence enhancement, since the nitrogen atoms are strong electron donors and can enhance the conjugated π-domains of Mg-N-CQDs [36]. Furthermore, the Mg(OH)2 can protect the carboxyl groups of citric acid during the procedure of dehydration and carbonization [32]. The amino/carboxyl groups are strong chelation groups, which have strong chelation with Mg2+ [37].

The UV–vis absorption spectra of Mg-CQDs, N-CQDs, and Mg-N-CQDs in aqueous solution are shown in Fig. 2 A, in which the inset is the photographs under natural light (left) and UV light (365nm, right). Both N-CQDs (curve b) and Mg-N-CQDs (curve c) have an absorption peak around 350nm, which is assigned to the n-π* transition, indicative of the aromatic π system with extended conjugation in the CQDs structures [32,38,39], where n and π* were from the CONH bond in aromatic structures. However, Mg-CQDs (curve a) almost have no absorption peak around 350nm. This is because the reaction of citric acid with Mg(OH)2 cannot form extended conjugation structures easily in the absence of EDA. As shown in the inset of Fig. 2A, Mg-CQDs do not emit fluorescence under UV light (365nm). However, Mg-N-CQDs show much stronger fluorescence than N-CQDs under UV light (365nm). The strong fluorescence emission of Mg-N-CQDs can be attributed to the radiative recombination of their surface-confined electron-hole pairs and the charge transfer of the surface-doped heteroatom to carbon [5,18].

The fluorescence emission spectra of the Mg-N-CQDs at different excitation wavelengths are presented in Fig. 2B. The emission peaks have no shift at different excitation wavelengths while most CQDs possess tunable fluorescence emissions, which indicate that Mg-N-CQDs may have a well-defined composition and structure, containing uniform surface state of sp2 clusters, thus leading to a high quantum yield [6,18,37]. The inset of Fig. 2B is the fluorescence excitation and emission spectra of Mg-N-CQDs in aqueous solution. When excited at the optimum excitation peak, 348nm, the Mg-N-CQDs exhibit strong fluorescence emission at 440nm.

Considering that the assay conditions, such as the concentration of Mg-N-CQDs, pH value and experiment time, have significant effect on the detection of Hg(II), so these conditions were optimized for better sensing performances. At first, the dosage of EDA and magnesium hydroxide for the synthesis of Mg-N-CQDs were optimized, 0.5μL of EDA and 0.16g of Mg(OH)2 were chosen as the optimum conditions (Fig. S7). The effect of the Mg-N-CQDs concentration was investigated (Fig. S8), showing that the quenching efficiency of Hg(II) was decreased with increasing concentration of Mg-N-CQDs, and 0.08mgmL−1 Mg-N-CQDs was chosen. Different pH values were studied in Fig. S9, the quenching efficiency of Mg-N-CQDs by Hg(II) is the highest at pH 5.33. The fluorescence intensities changed with pH values probably related to the carboxyl groups on the surface of Mg-N-CQDs [33,40]. At room temperature, when 15μM Hg(II) was introduced to the Mg-N-CQDs aqueous solution at pH 5.33, the fluorescence of Mg-N-CQDs was quenched rapidly and almost reached its minimum value after 10min. It is noticeable that the fluorescence of both Mg-N-CQDs/Hg(II) and Mg-N-CQDs systems remained stable for at least 2h at room temperature (Fig. S10A). The variance of fluorescence spectrum of Mg-N-CQDs/Hg(II) with increasing reaction time is displayed in Fig. S10B. Hence, 10min was enough for the system to reach the equilibrium.

In consideration of the appreciable changes in fluorescence intensity of the system in the presence of Hg(II), various concentrations of Hg(II) were added to Mg-N-CQDs aqueous solution and the fluorescence intensity was measured to study the sensitivity of Mg-N-CQDs for detection of Hg(II). The fluorescence intensity of Mg-N-CQDs was decreased gradually with increasing Hg(II) concentration (Fig. 3 A), which may be attributed to the electron-transfer process from the excited states of Mg-N-CQDs surface to the d orbital of Hg(II) [19,41,42]. Fig. 3B presents the relationship between the fluorescence intensity and the concentration of Hg(II). As illustrated in the inset of Fig. 3B, a linear equation ΔF = F 0 − F 1 =218.014 C +56.431 is observed in the concentration range from 0.05–5μM with a good linear correlation (R 2 =0.993). The detection limit for Hg(II) based on 3δ/slope was approximately 0.02μM. Compared to other fluorescent probes (Table S2), Mg-N-CQDs show a lower detection limit for Hg(II).

To evaluate the selectivity of the system, fifteen other metal ions, Ag(I), Al(III), Ca(II), Co(II), Cd(II), Cu(II), Fe(II), Fe(III), K(I), Mg(II), Mn(II), Na(I), Ni(II), Pb(II), and Zn(II), were chosen to measure their influence on the fluorescence intensity of the system. Although the concentration of Hg(II) ion was 5μM and that of other metal ions was 15μM, Hg(II) showed the strongest ability to quench the fluorescence of Mg-N-CQDs (Fig. 4 ). This may be attributed to the interaction of Mg-N-CQDs with Hg(II) and the formation of non-fluorescent complex. Whereas other metal ions have a little influence on the fluorescence of the system, illustrating that the system used to detect Hg(II) has a good selectivity.

After the fluorescence of Mg-N-CQDs aqueous solution was quenched by Hg(II), the addition of Cys made the fluorescence recovery. As shown in Fig. 5 , the effects of twenty essential amino acids on the fluorescence signal of Mg-N-CQDs/Hg(II) were investigated for comparison, only Cys exhibited notable recovery of the fluorescence. The effect of various amino acids on the fluorescence of Mg-N-CQDs/Hg(II) was described using (F 2 − F 1)/F 0. As can be seen from Fig. S11, the UV–vis absorption peak of Mg-N-CQDs/Hg(II) (curve b) has a red-shift compared to that of Mg-N-CQDs (curve a). However, with the addition of Cys, the absorption peak of Mg-N-CQDs/Hg(II)/Cys (curve c) was blue shifted to the same place of Mg-N-CQDs near 350nm. Consequently, we infer that the recovery was due to the presence of strong combination between Hg(II) and Cys, which formed Hg(II)S bonds and removed Hg(II) from the surface of Mg-N-CQDs [41]. Upon the addition of Cys to the aqueous solution of Mg-N-CQDs/Hg(II), the fluorescence of Mg-N-CQDs/Hg(II) was retrieved by degrees (Fig. S12A). The relationship of the fluorescence intensity of Mg-N-CQDs/Hg(II) versus the concentration of Cys is shown in Fig. S12B. There is a good linear relation (R 2 =0.994) in the range of 9–15μM described by the linear equation ΔF = F 2 − F 1 =201.493C −1691.986 (inset of Fig. S12B).

To investigate the practicality of the system, the Mg-N-CQDs sensing system was applied to the detection of Hg(II) in tap water and river water samples. The tap water was derived from our lab without any pretreatment. The river water sample obtained from Jialing River was filtered through a 200nm microporous membrane and centrifuged at 12,000rpm for 30min. Hg(II) was not found in tap water and river water samples. The recoveries of Hg(II) in real samples were then measured, which are in the range of 95.5–107.5% (Table 1 ). Moreover, the practical application of the Cys determination in human plasma was carried out. The human plasma sample was centrifuged at 6000rpm for 15min firstly, after that, 10μL of pretreated plasma spiked with various concentrations of Cys was added in the Mg-N-CQDs/Hg(II) system, good recoveries were obtained as shown in Table S3. These results indicated the reliability of our method for Hg(II) and Cys determination in real samples.

On the basis of the Hg(II)-and Cys-sensitive switching system mentioned above, an IMPLICATION logic gate using Hg(II) and Cys as inputs, the fluorescence signal of Mg-N-CQDs as output was designed. For inputs, the presence of Hg(II) or Cys were considered to be the “1” state and their absence as “0”. Only with the input of Hg(II) and without Cys (1/0), the fluorescence intensity (FL) of Mg-N-CQDs is low, and the output is “0” (off state). In all other cases, the outputs are “1” (on state). The truth Table (A) and symbol (B) of the IMPLICATION logic gate are given in Fig. 6 . The relative concentrations of Hg(II) and Cys in aqueous solutions can be reflected through this IMPLICATION logic gate, and the repeated switching behavior with alternating addition of Hg(II) and Cys is illustrated in Fig. 6C. After the cyclic treatment with Hg(II) and Cys, the outputs are gradually decreased.

In summary, the Mg-N-CQDs were synthesized with low precursor concentrations, however, the quantum yield and fluorescence of Mg-N-CQDs are very high. Based on the fact that the fluorescence of Mg-N-CQDs can be quenched in the presence of Hg(II), a sensor exhibiting good sensitivity and selectivity for Hg(II) detection was developed. After the addition of Cys to the Mg-N-CQDs/Hg(II) aqueous solution, Hg(II) reacted strongly with Cys, leading to the recovery of Mg-N-CQDs fluorescence. Thus, the system can be used for detection of Cys from twenty essential amino acids. Moreover, Mg-N-CQDs were successfully applied to the determination of Hg(II) in real water samples, suggesting its well potential applications for environmental analysis. Finally, an IMPLICATION logic gate was designed to mimic this experiment.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Nos. 20975083 and 21273174) and the Municipal Natural Science Foundation of Chongqing City (No. CSTC-2013jjB00002).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found, in the online version, at http://dx.doi.org/10.1016/j.snb.2016.02.141.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: The following are Supplementary data to this article:

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Electrophoretic analysis and purification of fluorescent single-walled carbon nanotube fragments
Quantum-sized carbon dots for bright and colorful photoluminescence
Photoluminescence properties of graphene versus other carbon nanomaterials
Functional surface engineering of C-dots for fluorescent biosensing and in vivo bioimaging
Carbon quantum dots and their applications
Carbon nanodots: synthesis, properties and applications
One-step synthesis of fluorescent carbon nanoparticles by laser irradiation
Hg(II) sensing based on functionalized carbon dots obtained by direct laser ablation
Nanosensor composed of nitrogen-doped carbon dots and gold nanoparticles for highly selective detection of cysteine with multiple signals
An electrochemical avenue to blue luminescent nanocrystals from multiwalled carbon nanotubes (MWCNTs)
Fluorescent carbon nanoparticles derived from candle soot
Fluorescent carbon nanoparticles: synthesis, characterization, and bioimaging application
Simple and sensitive fluorescent and electrochemical trinitrotoluene sensors based on aqueous carbon dots
Fluorescent carbon nanoparticles: electrochemical synthesis and their pH sensitive photoluminescence properties
Si-doped carbon quantum dots: a facile and general preparation strategy, bioimaging application, and multifunctional sensor
Carbon-based dots co-doped with nitrogen and sulfur for high quantum yield and excitation-independent emission
Nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots: facile synthesis and application as a turn-off fluorescent probe for detection of Hg2+ ions
Tools and tactics for the optical detection of mercuric ion
Fluorescent and colorimetric magnetic microspheres as nanosensors for Hg2+ in aqueous solution prepared by a sol–gel grafting reaction and host-guest interaction
Detection of mercury(II) by quantum dot/DNA/gold nanoparticle ensemble based nanosensor via nanometal surface energy transfer
Highly sensitive multiresponsive chemosensor for selective detection of Hg2+ in natural water and different monitoring environments
Facile synthesis of water-soluble fluorescent silver nanoclusters and HgII sensing
A general strategy to convert the MerR family proteins into highly sensitive and selective fluorescent biosensors for metal ions
Fluorescent Ag clusters via a protein-directed approach as a Hg(II) ion sensor
Fluorescence detection of mercury ions in aqueous media with the complex of a cationic oligopyrene derivative and oligothymine
Synthesis of highly fluorescent gold nanoparticles for sensing mercury(II)
Label-free colorimetric detection of aqueous mercury ion (Hg(II)) using Hg(II)-modulated G-quadruplex-based DNAzymes
Conjugate addition/cyclization sequence enables selective and simultaneous fluorescence detection of cysteine and homocysteine
Glutathione-protected silver nanoclusters as cysteine-selective fluorometric and colorimetric probe
Mg/N double doping strategy to fabricate extremely high luminescent carbon dots for bioimaging
Highly photoluminescent carbon dots for multicolor patterning sensors, and bioimaging
One-pot synthesis of N-doped carbon dots with tunable luminescence properties
Nanosized carbon particles from natural gas soot
Surface chemistry routes to modulate the photoluminescence of graphene quantum dots: from fluorescence mechanism to up-conversion bioimaging applications
Hexagonal cobalt oxyhydroxide-carbon dots hybridized surface: high sensitive fluorescence turn-on probe for monitoring of ascorbic acid in rat brain following brain ischemia
From metal-organic framework to intrinsically fluorescent carbon nanodots
One-pot and ultrafast synthesis of nitrogen and phosphorus co-doped carbon dots possessing bright dual wavelength fluorescence emission
Origin of strong excitation wavelength dependent fluorescence of graphene oxide
Carbon nanodots as fluorescence probes for rapid sensitive, and label-free detection of Hg(II) and biothiols in complex matrices
Use of surface-modified CdTe quantum dots as fluorescent probes in sensing mercury (II)

ce:biography.ce:simple-para: Ting Liu is a M.S. candidate in School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Southwest University, China. Her major research interest is spectrum analysis.
Na Li is a M.S. candidate in School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Southwest University, China. Her major research interest is spectrum analysis.
Jiang Xue Dong is a doctoral student in School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Southwest University, China. Her major research interest is spectrum analysis.

Hong Qun Luo is a professor of chemistry in School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Southwest University, China. She received her M.S. degree in environmental chemistry from Sichuan University in 1991 and Ph.D. degree in analytical chemistry from Southwest China Normal University in 2002. During 2006–2007, she was a Visiting Scholar in Tohoku University, Japan. Her research is focused on molecular spectroscopy and electrochemical sensors.

Nian Bing Li is a professor of chemistry in School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Southwest University, China. He received his M.S. degree in physical chemistry in 1997 and Ph.D. degree in material science in 2000 from Chongqing University. During 2000–2002, he was a Postdoctoral Research Fellow in Fuzhou University, China. Since 2006–2007, he was a Postdoctoral Research Fellow in Korea Advanced Institute of Science and Technology (KAIST), Korea. His research interests are the developments of electrochemical devices such as chemical sensors and biosensors.
